topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy brochure lay crumpled on the kitchen counter, the vibrant renderings of minimalist living mocking me. I stared at the tiny print detailing the mandatory upgrade fees, my jaw tight enough to crack a tooth. Every time I walked into the hallway, I felt a knot tighten in my chest, a dull, heavy pressure that made my shoulders hunch. I kept running my hand over the peeling paint on the elevator doors, seeking some solid ground in a world that suddenly felt like it was tilting sideways. "Another amenity fee," I muttered to my reflection in the darkened window, the sound thin and brittle. I slammed the brochure shut, the sharp sound echoing in the small living room, and paced three steps before stopping abruptly, breathing in short, shallow gasps.

[story 2]

"They're just going to take everything," I told my roommate, my voice dangerously level, though the tremor in my hands betrayed the internal turmoil. I kept gesturing wildly toward the floor plans spread across the coffee table, the lines and measurements seeming to mock my sense of stability. My eyes darted from the proposed marble countertops to the clause about mandatory association dues, and a hot flush crept up my neck. I picked up a heavy ceramic mug, gripping it so tightly my knuckles turned white, and slowly set it down on the worn wooden table. I didn't look at him, just stared at the ceiling, counting the seconds until I could find a way to make sense of this sudden, suffocating upheaval.

[story 3]

I spent an entire afternoon pacing the length of my narrow living room, the worn carpet fibers digging into the soles of my bare feet. The sheer volume of paperwork detailing the transition to a condominium structure was overwhelming; the pages blurred together under the harsh overhead light. I kept crumpling pieces of paper, the sound a sharp, involuntary release of tension. When the mail arrived, it was a thick envelope filled with legal jargon. I picked it up, my grip instinctively tightening until my fingers ached, and then I dropped it onto the floor with a heavy thud, the resulting silence amplifying the frantic thumping in my own ears. I leaned against the wall, closing my eyes, trying to force the swirling anxiety down, but the feeling just churned beneath my skin.
